question: A current of $4.36~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ions for $26~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Al}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Al} = 27.0~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 6794.31~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.0704~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $3~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.0704}{3} = 0.0235~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.0235)(27.0) \approx 0.63~\text{g}$.

question: Substance A has an enthalpy of vaporization of $33~\text{kJ/mol}$, while Substance B has an enthalpy of vaporization of $3~\text{kJ/mol}$. Approximately how many times stronger are the intermolecular forces in A compared to B?
explanation: Ratio $= \frac{33}{3} = 11$. This reflects the relative strength of the intermolecular forces.

question: Calculate the pH of a $0.05~\text{mol/dm}^3$ solution of a weak acid with $K_a = 1.3\times 10^{-03}$. Assume $[\text{HA}] \approx C_a$.
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $3193.46~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

A current of $1.48~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ ions for $58~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Cu}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Cu} = 63.5~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 5157.3~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.0535~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $2~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.0535}{2} = 0.0267~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.0267)(63.5) \approx 1.7~\text{g}$.

question: A $1.81~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $20~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.81}{40.0} = 0.0453~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0453$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0453}{0.02} = 2.27~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $5459.36~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

Light of frequency $1.39\times 10^{+15}~\text{Hz}$ shines on a metal with a work function of $2.47\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)

A $1.43~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $25~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.43}{40.0} = 0.0357~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0357$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0357}{0.025} = 1.43~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: A reaction has $\Delta H = -206~\text{kJ/mol}$. The total energy released by forming new bonds is $661~\text{kJ/mol}$. The reactants contain $1~\text{mol}$ of A-A bonds ($E = 127~\text{kJ/mol}$) and $2~\text{mol}$ of B-B bonds. Calculate the bond energy of the B-B bond.
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $5489.3~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

For the reaction $\text{A}(g) + \text{B}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{C}(g)$, the equilibrium constant is $K_c = 30$. If equilibrium concentrations of A and B are $0.84~\text{M}$ and $0.49~\text{M}$ respectively, calculate the equilibrium concentration of C.

In a titration, $10~\text{cm}^3$ of $0.1~\text{mol/dm}^3$ $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is exactly neutralized by $27.29~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{NaOH}$ solution. Calculate the concentration of the $\text{NaOH}$ solution.
A sample of a radioactive substance initially contains $477~\text{g}$. After $164~\text{days}$, $29.81~\text{g}$ remains. Calculate the half-life of the substance.
Calculate the volume occupied by $278.27~\text{g}$ of a gas at STP. Its molar mass is $111~\text{g/mol}$ and the molar volume of a gas at STP is $22.4~\text{dm}^3\text{/mol}$.
A $0.58~\text{dm}^3$ solution with a concentration of $0.04~\text{mol/dm}^3$ is diluted to a final volume of $2.85~\text{dm}^3$. Calculate the new concentration.
explanation: Using $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2 \Rightarrow (0.04)(0.58) = C_2(2.85) \Rightarrow C_2 = \frac{0.03}{2.85} = 0.01~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $5284.47~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

A reaction has $\Delta H = -398~\text{kJ/mol}$. The total energy released by forming new bonds is $601~\text{kJ/mol}$. The reactants contain $1~\text{mol}$ of A-A bonds ($E = 206~\text{kJ/mol}$) and $2~\text{mol}$ of B-B bonds. Calculate the bond energy of the B-B bond.
For the reaction $1\text{A} + 1\text{B} \rightarrow 1\text{C}$, you have $3.54~\text{mol}$ of A and $2.87~\text{mol}$ of B. Calculate the maximum moles of product C that can be formed.
explanation: The limiting reactant is B. Moles of C = $\frac{1}{1} \times 2.87 = 2.87~\text{mol}$.

question: For the reaction $\text{A}(g) + \text{B}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{C}(g)$, the equilibrium constant is $K_c = 33$. If equilibrium concentrations of A and B are $0.11~\text{M}$ and $0.77~\text{M}$ respectively, calculate the equilibrium concentration of C.
A metal has a work function of $3.90\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate its threshold frequency. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)
Calculate the number of molecules in $43.11~\text{g}$ of $\text{C}_{4}\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_{1}$. (Molar mass = $74.14~\text{g/mol}$, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}~\text{mol}^{-1}$)
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $12732.81~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

A $0.6~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $25~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{0.6}{40.0} = 0.015~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.015$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.015}{0.025} = 0.6~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: A current of $2.51~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ ions for $50~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Zn}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Zn} = 65.4~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 7518.38~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.0779~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $2~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.0779}{2} = 0.039~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.039)(65.4) \approx 2.55~\text{g}$.

question: A sample of a radioactive substance initially contains $445~\text{g}$. After $65~\text{days}$, $13.91~\text{g}$ remains. Calculate the half-life of the substance.

A current of $1.33~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ ions for $40~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Zn}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Zn} = 65.4~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 3187.06~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.033~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $2~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.033}{2} = 0.0165~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.0165)(65.4) \approx 1.08~\text{g}$.

question: For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, equilibrium concentrations are: $[\text{N}_2] = 0.65~\text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2] = 0.76~\text{M}$, $[\text{NH}_3] = 1.57~\text{M}$. Calculate $K_c$.
In a reaction, $35~\text{cm}^3$ of hydrogen gas is produced in $22~\text{s}$. Calculate the rate of production of hydrogen gas.
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $15208.39~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

A $1.95~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $10~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.95}{40.0} = 0.0486~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0486$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0486}{0.01} = 4.86~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: For the reaction $2\text{A} + 1\text{B} \rightarrow \text{Products}$, you have $2.85~\text{mol}$ of A and $1.15~\text{mol}$ of B. Calculate the moles of the excess reactant remaining after the reaction goes to completion.
explanation: Limiting is B. Moles of A used = $\frac{2}{1} \times 1.15 = 2.29~\text{mol}$. Remaining = $2.85 - 2.29 = 0.56~\text{mol}$.

question: Calculate the volume occupied by $140.55~\text{g}$ of a gas at STP. Its molar mass is $89~\text{g/mol}$ and the molar volume of a gas at STP is $22.4~\text{dm}^3\text{/mol}$.

In a titration, $25~\text{cm}^3$ of $0.1~\text{mol/dm}^3$ $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is exactly neutralized by $16.71~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{NaOH}$ solution. Calculate the concentration of the $\text{NaOH}$ solution.

For the reaction $\text{A}(g) + \text{B}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{C}(g)$, the equilibrium constant is $K_c = 13$. If equilibrium concentrations of A and B are $0.29~\text{M}$ and $0.83~\text{M}$ respectively, calculate the equilibrium concentration of C.
In a reaction, $25~\text{cm}^3$ of hydrogen gas is produced in $36~\text{s}$. Calculate the rate of production of hydrogen gas.
Calculate the mass of $0.68~\text{moles}$ of $\text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{6}$. (Molar mass = $30.08~\text{g/mol}$)
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $18756.23~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

A $0.99~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $25~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{0.99}{40.0} = 0.0247~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0247$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0247}{0.025} = 0.99~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: A solution has a pH of $3.1$. Calculate the hydrogen ion concentration, $[\text{H}^+]$.
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $10114.91~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
Calculate the number of molecules in $84.06~\text{g}$ of $\text{C}_{5}\text{H}_{12}$. (Molar mass = $72.17~\text{g/mol}$, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}~\text{mol}^{-1}$)

A $1.49~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $20~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.49}{40.0} = 0.0372~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0372$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0372}{0.02} = 1.86~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: In a titration, $25~\text{cm}^3$ of $0.2~\text{mol/dm}^3$ $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is exactly neutralized by $21.44~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{NaOH}$ solution. Calculate the concentration of the $\text{NaOH}$ solution.
In a chemical reaction, the sum of bond energies for bonds broken is $312~\text{kJ/mol}$ and for bonds formed is $400~\text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) for the reaction.
The electronegativity difference between Atom P and Atom Q is $1.3$. If Atom P is the more electronegative atom with an electronegativity of $4$, what is the electronegativity of Atom Q?

For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, equilibrium concentrations are: $[\text{N}_2] = 0.2~\text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2] = 0.18~\text{M}$, $[\text{NH}_3] = 0.88~\text{M}$. Calculate $K_c$.

For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, equilibrium concentrations are: $[\text{N}_2] = 0.19~\text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2] = 0.44~\text{M}$, $[\text{NH}_3] = 1.15~\text{M}$. Calculate $K_c$.

For the reaction $\text{A}(g) + \text{B}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{C}(g)$, the equilibrium constant is $K_c = 73$. If equilibrium concentrations of A and B are $0.17~\text{M}$ and $0.27~\text{M}$ respectively, calculate the equilibrium concentration of C.

A $1.1~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $10~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.1}{40.0} = 0.0276~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0276$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0276}{0.01} = 2.76~\text{mol/dm}^3$.
